chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing complicated measles in SAM patients, including infection control, vitamin A dosing, and supportive care to prevent complications like pneumonia and corneal injury.
learning_objectives: Understand the management priorities for complicated measles in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Learn the appropriate vitamin A dosing regimen for children with measles and SAM based on age.
Recognize the importance of infection control and empiric antibiotic use in complicated SAM cases.
Identify key discharge tasks, including immunization planning and follow-up care.
key_facts: Malnourished children with measles are at higher risk for pneumonia, dehydration, and corneal injury.
Vitamin A dosing: 50,000 IU (<6 months), 100,000 IU (6–12 months), 200,000 IU (>12 months).
Avoid routine IV boluses for dehydration unless shock criteria are met; use malnutrition-appropriate oral/NG rehydration.
Empiric antibiotics are recommended for suspected bacterial superinfections in complicated SAM cases.
Anti-staphylococcal coverage is critical in post-measles pneumonia or extensive skin infections.
Discharge tasks include infection control, documenting measles vaccination status, and planning follow-up doses.
reference_quote: Malnourished children have higher risk of severe or complicated measles, including pneumonia, diarrhea with dehydration, and corneal injury. (WHO, Pocket book of hospital care for children)
document_key_topics: Infection control and empiric antibiotic use
